statement.markdown:
Given an array of intervals, merge all overlapping intervals to produce a new array of non-overlapping intervals that cover the same regions.

For example, given the intervals `[1, 3]`, `[2, 6]`, `[8, 10]`, and `[15, 18]`, the merged intervals are `[1, 6]`, `[8, 10]`, and `[15, 18]`.

The input intervals may not be in any particular order, and the intervals may have different lengths.

The goal is to write a function that takes the input intervals and returns the merged intervals.
statement.inputFormat:
The number of intervals
For each interval: the start and end values
statement.outputFormat:
The number of merged intervals
For each merged interval: the start and end values

constraints.description: The start and end values of each interval

examples.explanation: The intervals [1, 3] and [2, 6] overlap, so they are merged into [1, 6]. The other intervals do not overlap, so they are left unchanged.
The intervals [1, 4] and [4, 5] overlap, so they are merged into [1, 5]. The interval [6, 7] does not overlap with the previous intervals.

hints.text: The problem involves intervals on a number line. Think about how you can sort them in a way that makes merging easier.
Consider sorting the intervals by their start value. This could allow you to iterate through them in order and merge as you go.

The key to solving this problem is to recognize that if the current interval overlaps with the last merged interval, you can merge them by updating the end of the last merged interval. If not, you add the current interval to the list of merged intervals.

editorial.title: Merging Intervals with Efficiency
editorial.intuition: The problem requires merging overlapping intervals. A brute force approach would involve checking every interval against every other, which is highly inefficient. However, sorting the intervals by their start value provides a crucial observation: only adjacent intervals need to be compared to check for overlaps.
editorial.bruteForce: A brute force approach would involve iterating through each interval and comparing it with every other interval to check for overlaps. This would result in a time complexity of O(n^2), which is inefficient for large inputs.
editorial.betterSolution: The optimal approach involves sorting the intervals by their start value and then iterating through them, merging any overlapping intervals.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution involves sorting the intervals, which takes O(n log n) time, and then iterating through them once, which takes O(n) time. This results in an overall time complexity of O(n log n) and a space complexity of O(n) for storing the merged intervals.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the optimal solution can be proven by observing that sorting the intervals ensures that any overlapping intervals will be adjacent. By iterating through the sorted intervals and merging any overlaps, we ensure that all intervals are merged correctly. The use of a max function to update the end value of the merged interval ensures that the merged interval is correct even when there are multiple overlapping intervals.
editorial.dryRun: For example, given the intervals [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]], sorting them results in [[1,3],[2,6],[8,10],[15,18]]. Iterating through them and merging overlaps results in [[1,6],[8,10],[15,18]], which is the correct result.
commonMistakes: Not sorting the intervals, leading to incorrect merging
Not checking for overlaps between adjacent intervals
Not updating the end value of the merged interval correctly
Not handling edge cases such as empty input or intervals with negative values
Assuming that the input intervals are already sorted
Not using a efficient sorting algorithm, leading to poor performance
